questions.text: I sense when a group has collectively decided on something before any formal vote or discussion.
I find that my body holds onto the physical signature of an emotion long after my mind has moved on.
I find that I cannot simply turn off my concern for someone once I have seen their pain.
When I feel gratitude, it swells inside me until I can barely contain it.
I feel that my creative projects deserve the same level of investment I would give to a professional obligation.
I build new skills through immersion and play rather than deliberate drill.
I systematically review and update my plans as new information becomes available.
I keep communication threads organized so I can retrieve past discussions easily.
I feel that my emotional range extends further in both directions than most people's.
I think that analytical composure during distressing events allows me to act in ways that are both effective and fair.
I find that putting my own projects first leads to my best outcomes.
I am someone who embraces and strengthens a shared mission.
I believe that the moral priority of suffering is the insight that connects abstract ethics to the concrete reality of human lives.
I hold the view that maintaining objectivity when others are in distress is the foundation of sound judgment in any helping profession.
I tend to understate my own needs so as not to divert attention from others.
I do not pick up on unspoken hierarchies in social groups.
I routinely dedicate my best hours to my own creative and professional projects.
I am the one who typically helps the group settle on the key question together.
I find that I am usually the last to sense when a social norm has been broken.
I am the one who decides when to pivot and when to stay the course.
I tend to process grief in a measured, gradual way rather than in sudden, overwhelming waves.
I pick up on when someone shortens their responses as a signal they want the conversation to end.
I believe that the moral significance of suffering is absolute — it does not depend on context, timing, or the identity of the person involved.
I hold the view that unused privilege is wasted privilege.
I routinely offer to handle the least glamorous tasks in any group effort.
I experience the feeling of being startled as a brief flash that dissipates almost immediately.
I tend to feel curious about my emotions rather than overtaken by them.
I take a methodical approach to learning new skills, following a structured curriculum.
I feel that the effort of maintaining strict order often outweighs the benefits.
I tend to spend money based on what feels right in the moment rather than following a budget.
I am more inclined to formulate a plan of action than to dwell on the emotional dimensions of a difficult situation.
I do not pick up on the nonverbal signals that indicate whether a group wants my input.
I feel energized when I single-handedly turn a situation around.
I feel that meetings can be valuable even without concrete outcomes, as long as the group connected.
I frequently introduce new initiatives without waiting for a mandate.
I notice when someone's eye contact becomes slightly more intense because they want to be believed.
